title: Quantitative Assessment of the Readability of Patient Education Material by the Society of Vascular Surgery
short_title: Quantitative Assessment of the Readability of Patient Education Material by the Society of Vascular Surgery
authors: Hakimi A, Othman L, Soucy JW, Chatzis KD, Zil-E-Ali A, Ali T, Aziz F
affiliations: Division of Vascular Surgery, Penn State Milton S. Hershey Medical Center; Penn State College of Medicine, Hershey, PA

abstract.Objectives: Health literacy is a key determinant of patient outcomes. Patient education materials (PEMs) are intended to improve health literacy. The NIH and AMA recommend that PEMs be written at or below an eighth-grade and sixth-grade reading level, respectively. Despite these recommendations, repeated studies demonstrate that most PEMs are written at significantly higher reading levels, limiting their effectiveness. The purpose of this study was to evaluate the readability of publicly available PEMs from the Society for Vascular Surgery (SVS).
abstract.Methods: Fifteen SVS PEMs were identified and downloaded from the SVS website and converted to plain text. Their readability was assessed using 6 validated indices: Flesch-Kincaid Grade Level, Flesch Reading Ease (FRE), Gunning-Fog Index, Coleman-Liau Index, Automated Readability Index, and Simple Measure of Gobbledygook (SMOG). Descriptive statistics, ANOVA, Kruskal-Wallis, and Cohen's d were used to compare results against NIH/AMA benchmarks and assess differences across indices.
abstract.Results: All 15 SVS PEMs were written above the recommended sixth-grade reading level (P<0.01), and 12 exceeded the eighth-grade reading level (P<0.05). Despite significant variation between metrics, all indices produced consistent grade-level estimates and showed average readability above sixth grade. The mean readability grade levels across the 6 indices ranged from 8.63 to 14.56, with cerebrovascular disease, arterial dissection, and deep venous thrombosis being the most difficult to understand. There were statistically significant variations between metrics (ANOVA P<0.0001; Kruskal-Wallis P<0.0001), with an Eta-squared analysis suggesting 30.6% of the variance in readability scores was attributable to the choice of readability calculator. FRE scores categorized 73% of PEMs as 'fairly difficult' or 'very difficult.' The SMOG index produced the lowest grade levels while the Coleman-Liau index produced the highest (difference = 3.33 grade levels).
abstract.Conclusions: PEMs from the SVS consistently exceed the readability thresholds recommended by the NIH and AMA, mirroring trends in other specialties. Poorly designed PEMs can exacerbate disparities in health literacy and worsen outcomes in an already sick population. These results underscore the need for the SVS to improve patient handouts to better align with national readability standards.

takeaways: First dedicated audit of the Society for Vascular Surgery's patient education materials: all 15 SVS PEMs exceeded the AMA-recommended 6th-grade reading level (p<0.01), and 12 of 15 also exceeded the NIH 8th-grade level (p<0.05).

Mean grade levels per handout ranged from 8.63 (Thoracic Outlet Syndrome — the most readable) to 14.56 (Cerebrovascular Disease); the hardest five were cerebrovascular disease, arterial dissection, deep vein thrombosis, chronic kidney disease/dialysis, and aortic dissection.
The choice of readability calculator mattered: ANOVA p<0.0001 and Kruskal-Wallis p<0.0001 across indices, with Eta-squared (η²=0.306) attributing 30.6% of score variance to which calculator was used.

SMOG produced the lowest mean grade level (9.69) and Coleman-Liau the highest (13.02) — a 3.33 grade-level spread — yet all five grade-level indices agreed every PEM was above threshold (inter-index Pearson r 0.70–0.95).
Flesch Reading Ease scores ranged 25–64 across the 15 PEMs, and 73% were categorized as 'Fairly Difficult' or 'Very Difficult.'

SVS PEMs sit in the same range as audits of AHA (mean FKGL 10.22), ACC (9.86), and ESVS (mean grade 10.61) materials — a society-wide problem; the authors call for a dedicated SVS subcommittee to revise PEMs to health-literacy standards.

figures.caption: Figure 1: Readability grade level heatmap for FKGL, SMOG, Gunning-Fog, Coleman-Liau, and Adjusted Readability Index calculators for 15 available Society for Vascular Surgery patient education handouts. The five most difficult handouts, Cerebrovascular Disease, Arterial Dissection, Deep Vein Thrombosis, Chronic Kidney Disease and Dialysis, and Aortic Dissection, consistently score the highest across all metrics.

figures.title: Distribution of grade levels across readability calculators
figures.caption: Figure 2: Boxplot distribution of average grade levels for all PEM's per readability index, highlighting systematic differences in estimated grade levels. ANOVA and Kruskal-Wallis p ≤ 0.0001.

figures.caption: Supplementary Figure 2: Correlation heatmap between readability metrics, demonstrating strong agreement among indices, with pairwise Pearson coefficients ranging from 0.70 to 0.95.

figures.title: Flesch Reading Ease score by handout
figures.caption: Supplementary Figure 3: The Flesch Reading Ease (FRE) Score provides an inverse relationship calculation to demonstrate difficulty of reading. Lower scores suggest difficult to read, higher scores suggest easier to read. Most SVS handouts fall under the Difficult or Fairly Difficult categories.

ANOVA p<0.0001; Kruskal-Wallis p<0.0001; Eta-squared η²=0.306 (30.6% of variance attributable to calculator choice). Inter-index Pearson correlations ranged 0.70–0.95.

sections.title: Introduction
paras: Access to clear and understandable health information is essential for patients to make informed decisions about their care. However, the complexity of medical information often prohibits many patients from fully comprehending their diagnoses, risk factors, the importance of routine screening, and treatment options. Health literacy, which includes a person's ability to seek, comprehend, and apply health information, has been shown to be a modifiable risk factor and mediator of socioeconomic status in health disparities as well as one of the strongest predictors of health outcomes[1-3]. To help address the challenges with health literacy, Patient Education Material (PEM) including websites, handouts, and pamphlets are designed to simplify complex medical concepts. However, many PEMs are non-standardized and as a result often written at a complexity level that exceeds the health literacy of most patients. The average reading level of patients in the United States is around the 8th grade[4, 5], which is why organizations including the National Institutes of Health (NIH) and the American Medical Association (AMA) recommend PEMs be written at a 6th-grade level[6].

Patients suffering from vascular disease represent a challenging situation, as a majority are older, have several coexisting comorbidities, and face complex decision-making trees for management of their multifaceted disease processes. Review of the literature[7-13] shows that most patients struggle to understand many of the online educational materials for vascular diseases. Therefore, the Society for Vascular Surgery (SVS) has taken the responsibility of producing PEMs for vascular surgery patients that are written in an easily understandable language. These materials are freely and readily accessible to the public on the SVS website[14]. The ease of understanding of SVS PEMs has not been assessed objectively. Review of the literature analyzing PEMs published across different medical and surgical specialties shows that in general, PEMs exceed the recommended reading level[15-17]. A review by Rooney et al. in 2021 evaluated the readability of 2585 PEMs published in high-impact medical journals and found only 2.1% of materials met the recommended 6th-grade reading level[18]. More recently, Okuhara et al. performed a systematic review including over 29,424 PEMs across 438 studies and report that despite continued research, readability levels have not improved over the last 20+ years[19].

Given the essential role of health literacy in patient outcomes, it is imperative to evaluate and improve the readability of PEMs. As the leader in the management of vascular conditions, the SVS provides PEMs on all major vascular disorders. While individual SVS PEMs may be incidentally encapsulated in other studies, to our knowledge there has yet to be any collective reviews specifically assessing the quality of the educational materials distributed by our society. This study aims to fill this void by utilizing validated readability indices to assess the readability of the 15 different PEMs provided by the SVS.

sections.title: Methods
paras: A total of 15 patient education pamphlets were downloaded from the Society for Vascular Surgery (SVS) public health flyers website in April 2025[14]. These documents represent the complete set of patient education materials provided by the SVS and cover the full spectrum of vascular conditions, including abdominal aortic aneurysm, carotid artery disease, deep vein thrombosis, peripheral artery disease, varicose and spider veins, acute limb ischemia, aortic dissection, arterial dissection, cerebrovascular disease, chronic kidney disease and dialysis, chronic venous insufficiency, May-Thurner syndrome, pelvic congestion syndrome, renal and mesenteric artery occlusive disease, and thoracic outlet syndrome.

Each document was converted to a plain text Microsoft Word document. Following the approach of Kue et al.[20], any institutional or brand-specific logos and headers were removed from the text while preserving all medically relevant content, symbols, and punctuation. The cleaned text from each brochure was then scored using six different readability assessments. These indices are established standards in health literacy research, and each yields an estimate of the education level needed to understand the material[20]. Readability scores were calculated using the free online tool available at ReadabilityFormulas.com, which allowed for consistent calculation of each index[2, 20]. The six readability calculators are defined in Table 1 and included: Flesch-Kincaid Grade Level (FKGL), Flesch Reading Ease (FRE), Simple Measure of Gobbledygook (SMOG), Gunning-Fog Index (GFI), Coleman-Liau Index (CLI), and Automated Readability Index (ARI).

For each educational document, readability scores from all six indices were recorded and compared to established benchmarks recommended by the AMA and the NIH, which suggest that patient education materials should be written at or below a 6th-grade or 8th-grade reading level respectively[6]. While Kue et al.[20] primarily utilized three indices and focused on a 6th-grade cutoff, we expanded our approach to incorporate three additional measures (GFI, CLI, and ARI). Using multiple established readability formulas helped ensure greater reliability, as each formula calculates readability using different parameters: sentence length, syllable counts, polysyllabic words, complex word percentages, or character-to-word ratios.

Statistical analysis was performed including descriptive statistics with mean grade level per handout, standard deviation, and 95% confidence interval, with p values less than .05 considered significant when comparing against 6th- and 8th-grade readability levels, as well as a Cohen's d value to assess differences in mean. We also performed an analysis of variance (ANOVA) as well as Kruskal-Wallis for nonparametric testing, similar to the previous work of Avra et al.[9]. Given the quality improvement and retrospective design of our study without human subjects, institutional review board approval was not required.

sections.title: Results
paras: The mean readability grade levels across the 15 SVS educational documents exceeded the 6th-grade threshold (p<0.01), and 12 of 15 exceeded the 8th-grade threshold (p<0.05), as summarized in Table 1. Mean scores ranged from 8.63 (Thoracic Outlet Syndrome) to 14.56 (Cerebrovascular Disease). Three documents — Thoracic Outlet Syndrome, Varicose and Spider Veins, and Carotid Artery Disease — did not reach statistical significance relative to the 8th-grade benchmark, though all three remained above the 6th-grade threshold.

The grade-level estimates varied across the five different grade-level indices and are illustrated in the readability heatmap (Figure 1). The TOS handout consistently scored the lowest across all indices (FKGL = 7.72; SMOG = 8.06). The five most difficult handouts across all metrics were Cerebrovascular Disease (mean 14.56), Arterial Dissection (14.0), Deep Vein Thrombosis (13.43), Chronic Kidney Disease and Dialysis (12.81), and Aortic Dissection (12.66).

Figure 2 illustrates that statistically significant variations were observed between readability calculators (ANOVA p<0.0001; Kruskal-Wallis p<0.0001). The Coleman-Liau Index produced the highest mean scores (13.02), while SMOG consistently yielded lower mean estimates (9.69). Eta-squared analysis (η² = 0.306) suggested that 30.6% of variance in readability scores was attributable to the choice of which calculator was used. Despite this variability, all five indices converged in classifying every PEM as above the recommended thresholds. Detailed grade-level estimates for each individual document across all five indices are presented in Supplementary Figure 1, while Pearson correlation coefficients showing strong inter-index agreement ranging from 0.70 to 0.95 are shown in Supplementary Figure 2.

Flesch Reading Ease (FRE) scores, which provide an inverse measure of reading difficulty on a 0-100 scale, ranged from 25-64 across the 15 PEMs (Supplementary Figure 3). Of the 15 documents, 73% were categorized as 'Fairly Difficult' or 'Very Difficult.' No consistent association was observed between document length and reading difficulty.

sections.title: Discussion
paras: In general, patients with vascular diseases are often medically complex and face a high disease burden by the time they present for management, and there is a crucial role for better understanding the disease process, the importance of lifestyle modifications, and conservative management in the initial stages of most vascular disease processes[21, 22]. These challenges are compounded by the fact that vascular conditions disproportionately affect older adults, many of whom may experience cognitive decline, visual impairment, or limited health literacy[23, 24]. Our findings build upon existing literature that has consistently shown patient education materials from vascular surgery and across other specialties exceed recommended readability levels. Scott et al.[8] analyzed content on abdominal aortic aneurysm operations from the top 10 websites on Google and Bing and found the average reading grade level for all sites was 12.8, with none written at or below the recommended sixth-grade level. Likewise, qualitative analysis by Bailey et al.[7] of abdominal aortic aneurysm content on Google, Yahoo!, and Bing yielded poor readability scores on all websites. Similarly, online searches on Google for peripheral arterial disease by Avra et al.[9] identified 25 patient-accessible articles whose average readability was higher than the recommended sixth-grade level. All the published evidence clearly shows that available online educational resources for patients with vascular diagnoses are not written at the recommended sixth-grade level.

In addition, our findings are consistent with those reported for PEMs from other major medical societies. Published readability audits of the American Heart Association (AHA) online patient materials report a mean Flesch-Kincaid Grade Level of 10.22 ± 1.19, while the American College of Cardiology (ACC) materials average a mean FKGL of 9.86 ± 1.42[25, 26]. Similarly, patient-facing content from the European Society for Vascular Surgery (ESVS) has been reported at a mean reading grade level of 10.61 (range 6.4-14.5)[27]. Our SVS materials fall within a similar range, underscoring that the challenge of producing accessible PEMs is a society-wide problem not unique to vascular surgery.

To the best of the authors' knowledge, this is the first focused analysis of the Society for Vascular Surgery's Patient Education Materials. As one of the premier leaders for vascular care across the world, content produced by the SVS is often considered 'gold standard' and therefore has potential for significant impact in patient care. All 15 PEMs assessed exceeded the 6th-grade reading level, and we identified five materials with the highest average reading grade level: cerebrovascular disease (14.55), arterial dissection (14.08), deep vein thrombosis (13.44), chronic kidney disease and dialysis (12.92), and aortic dissection (12.8). The lowest calculated grade was for thoracic outlet syndrome (8.61), which, despite being the most readable, still surpassed the recommended level.

The discussion of complex vascular conditions often requires the inclusion of urgent, life-threatening scenarios, interrelated organ systems, or specialized terminology that is difficult to avoid without sacrificing accuracy. For example, the PEM addressing chronic kidney disease and dialysis must encompass metabolic derangements, vascular access, renal function, and dialysis modalities. Although balancing medical accuracy with readability has been shown to be challenging, changes to shorter sentence structure, simpler language, and fewer polysyllabic words can lead to significant improvements in readability scores. In Figure 3, we illustrate how such changes in two sections of the CKD handout can lead to more appropriate levels, with an average grade level of 6.44. However, the simplification of medical texts, although possible, can lead patients to overestimate their understanding of the material[28]; as such, an emphasis must also be placed on assessing patient understanding.

This analysis is not without limitations. It was restricted to the 15 PEMs available on the SVS website at the time of data collection. Although limiting our analysis to a single source reduced variability in authorship and formatting, it inherently constrained the generalizability of our findings. Another limitation is that readability indices cannot assess non-textual components such as figures or diagrams, which may significantly enhance comprehension[30]. Additionally, our study focused solely on readability, not comprehension. Without patient-centered testing, we cannot draw firm conclusions about how effectively these materials communicate information. Further investigation should incorporate patient-centered assessments, such as interviews, focus groups, or validated tools like the Patient Education Materials Assessment Tool (PEMAT). We suggest the formation of a dedicated subcommittee within the SVS to standardize, evaluate, and revise PEMs in accordance with health literacy guidelines[31].

sections.title: Conclusion
paras: Poorly designed patient education materials can create significant barriers to understanding, especially among patients with complex vascular disease. This study evaluated the readability of 15 PEMs from the SVS and found that all exceeded the recommended health literacy standards. Identifying gaps between current readability levels and national recommendations can guide improvements in patient education material development. Our findings underscore the need for increased attention to improving health literacy in vascular education materials. Future studies should focus on revising PEMs using plain language strategies and the impact of appropriately written educational materials on parameters such as patient satisfaction, adherence, and clinical outcomes.
